title: Concurrent Exhibitions at List Gallery
description:
Join us for two concurrent exhibitions at the List Gallery: Paper Journeys: Pop-up Books and Constructions by Colette Fu and Views of Travel, Reverie, and Repose: Selected East Asian Works from the Swarthmore College Art Collection. Together, these exhibitions explore current and historical East Asian works and themes, such as culture and identity, travel and its transformations, and the importance of diverse narratives. Paper Journeys features mixed-media works by Colette Fu, a Philadelphia-based artist who is known for her paper constructions that explore her Nuosu Yi ancestry and the cultural traditions of diverse ethnic minority groups in China. Views of Travel, Reverie, and Repose features works from Swarthmore's College Art Collection and highlights concerns explored by East Asian artists from the 14th century to the mid-twentieth century. These interrelated exhibitions were co-curated and organized by Andrea Packard, Director of the Swarthmore College Art Collection and Curator of the List Gallery; Sara Hesdon Buehler, Art Collection Manager; and Susan Eberhard, Visiting Assistant Professor, Art History.

The exhibitions will be on view in the List Gallery from March 3 through April 19, 2026.
Colette Fu will give a talk about her work on Thursday, March 19 at 4:30 PM in the Lang Performing Arts Center Cinema; an opening reception will follow in the List Gallery from 5:30 to 7:00 PM.

title: ENVS Colloquium Series Talk MacKay Jenkins
description:
Environmental Studies Program 2026 Colloquium Series

 
Talk by: McKay Jenkins    

 

Date:  Monday, April 6th, 2026
 
Time:  12:30 pm - 1:30 pm (please note the changed time!) 

 

(Bring your lunch)

 
Location:  SCI Cunniff (SC 199)

Title:  "The Praxis of Environmental Justice: Working Ethically with University Students and Community Partners"


For the past twenty years, my University of Delaware students have been training in environmental and community engagement efforts, working with regional Black and Native American partners. This includes field-based research in everything from the cultural and historical (learning structures of economic disempowerment and environmental degradation) to clinical techniques of ecological restoration work (building community greenhouses; growing food on urban farms; learning to identify native and invasive plants; learning to repair of garden soils) to the creative and scholarly (how to create pieces of journalism, advocacy writing, or grant proposals). South of UD’s campus, Baltimore City offers broad opportunities to study, support and learn from community work in ecological restoration and community building, especially through urban reforestation and food security.



McKay Jenkins is a writer, professor, restoration ecologist and urban farmer. He has published nine books and many articles and essays about the natural world and social justice. His book The Maryland Naturalist (Johns Hopkins University Press, 2024) is a collection of natural history essays that serves as the primary text for the Maryland State Master Naturalist Certification Program. His book The Delaware Naturalist Handbook (University of Delaware Press, 2020, co-edited with Susan Barton), serves as the primary text for the Delaware State Master Naturalist Certification Program. Jenkins other books include Food Fight: GMOs and the Future of the American Diet (Avery, 2017); Poison Spring: The Secret History of Pollution and the EPA (co-authored with Evaggelos Vallianatos) (Bloomsbury, 2014); and ContamiNation: My Quest to Survive in a Toxic World (Avery, 2016), originally published as What’s Gotten Into Us: Staying Healthy in a Toxic World (Random House, 2011), which chronicled his investigation into the myriad synthetic chemicals we encounter in our daily lives, and the growing body of evidence about the harm these chemicals do to our bodies and the environment.


 

Jenkins holds degrees from Amherst, Columbia’s Graduate School of Journalism, and Princeton, where he received a PhD in English. A former staff writer for the Atlanta Constitution, he has also written for Outside, Orion, The New Republic, and many other publications. Jenkins is currently the Cornelius Tilghman Professor of English, Journalism and Environmental Humanities at UD, where he has won the University Excellence in Teaching Award and both the Excellence in Teaching Award and the Outstanding Scholarship Award from the College of Arts and Sciences. He oversees the Rock Rose Food Justice Project, an urban farm in Baltimore, where he lives with his family.



 

Co-Sponsored by the Environmental Studies Program and the Mellon Foundation

title: Building Trauma-Informed Campus Support for Survivors (Immigrant-Informed)
description: This is a collaboration with the Nationalities Service Center (NSC),  a Philadelphia nonprofit that provides trauma-informed services to immigrant and refugee communities, including survivors of violence and other forms of trauma. The event delivers a trauma-informed campus support workshop that equips students, faculty, and staff with practical tools for responding to disclosures of violence and connecting survivors to appropriate resources. This programming is especially timely given the recent increase in Title IX–related concerns across campus and the corresponding need for clearer, more survivor-centered support practices in everyday campus spaces. While the workshop is open to all survivors of violence, it includes an immigrant-informed lens to address cultural and structural barriers (e.g., language access, stigma, and fear of systems) that can shape help-seeking and safety.

description: The Arabic Language Table provides a relaxed, supportive space for students to practice conversational Arabic, build confidence, and engage with the language beyond the classroom. By creating a sense of community, it connects students across proficiency levels, motivating them to participate and apply their skills in real-world interactions. This informal setting helps bridge classroom learning with practical use, preparing students for study abroad, internships, and conversations with native speakers. Ultimately, it enhances fluency, cultural awareness, and a deeper appreciation for the Arabic language.

title: Writing Cross Cultures, Working Between Languages: The Translational Prism of Taiwan
description:
This talk explores the intricate histories and human connections embedded in contemporary Taiwanese literature—works that strive to reclaim forbidden memories and navigate the identity of a multilingual,
immigrant society. Using translation as a "prism," we will examine the diverse facets of Taiwanese culture and the power of literary translation to capture the subtle nuances of shifting linguistic practices.

Featuring Lin King Lin King is an acclaimed writer and translator from Taiwan. A 2018 winner of the PEN/Robert J. Dau Short Story Prize, she reached a career milestone in 2024 as a winner of the National Book Award for Translated Literature. Joining us to discuss the challenges and promises of the craft, she will focus on three of her major translated works: Taiwan Travelogue: A Novel, The Boy from Clearwater, and A Perfect Day to Put Your Head into the Oven.

Sponsored by Modern Languages and Literatures (Chinese, Japanese and Russian Sections), Asian Studies, Comparative Literature and Global Studies Contact: Claire Li; cli3@swarthmore.edu
location: Kohlberg Hall Scheuer Room - Multi-Purpose Room

title: “Storying the ‘Land of Fires’: Feminist and Disabled Political Ecologies of Environmental Justice in Southern Italy”
description:
“Storying the ‘Land of Fires’: Feminist and Disabled Political Ecologies of Environmental Justice in Southern Italy”

Dr. Ilenia Iengo, Postdoctoral Researcher, Disabled Ecologies Lab, UC Berkeley

In this talk, Iengo explores her scholar-activist approach to feminist and disabled political ecological praxis, weaving together knowledges, practices, and imaginaries emerging from social and environmental justice organizing in the ‘Land of Fires’ (Terra dei Fuochi) in Campania, Italy.  ‘Land of Fires’ refers to the region in southern Italy that has been the site of decades of illegal toxic dumping and burning, resulting in air and water contamination and disproportionately high rates of cancer, reproductive disorders, and other environmental illnesses.  Grounded in the lived experiences of sacrificed and marginalized communities, Iengo argues that storytelling becomes a powerful tool for political organizing and consciousness-raising. It invites us to critically examine the ableist, heteronormative, and neoliberal frameworks that shape dominant understandings of health, nature, and justice, while also opening space for the transformative possibilities of collective care, mutual aid, and interdependence among bodies and territories.

title: GEO Cape Town Consortium Information Session
description:
The Consortium Program, established in 2003, offers students of Macalester, Pomona, and Swarthmore Colleges (and their affiliated institutions) the opportunity to study the environment and complex, multi-cultural society of South Africa against a background of globalization and rapid technological change. The program takes place at the University of Cape Town, a world-class institution where professors, in consultation with those from the consortium colleges, have developed a curriculum that meets the high academic standards of our schools while at the same time facilitating the challenge of living and studying in a foreign country. The program is hosted by the Department of Environmental and Geographical Science (EGS) at the University of Cape Town (UCT), which has an internationally respected student-faculty team with strong areas of interest in issues such as environmental and climate change, Global Information Systems, urban and agricultural development, and conservation of biodiversity. Cape Town itself is a breathtakingly beautiful city nestled against Table Mountain at the southwestern tip of Africa.

 

This is a spring only program.

 

Join us for Q&A with Prof. Carr Everbach, Andie D'Agostino, Assistant Director in GEO, and returned study abroad students.

Written by British playwright Alistair McDowall and directed by Montana Hamel ‘26, POMONA is a witty, macabre meditation on the gruesome underbelly of the corrupt society we inhabit, which illuminates the sacrifice and humanity that bind us together. As a one-act play, it confronts the viewer with a non-chronological narrative that highlights the tragedy of each character’s connection to Pomona, a concrete hole in the middle of the city that is home to the unseen horrors of the elite who profit from black market trade. For only three nights this April, come solve the puzzle of Pomona for yourself, and watch the characters of the play fall victim to the loop of fate as they navigate their connections to Pomona, the law, the Great Old Ones, and themselves.

title: "AI and the Future of Humanity" : A Night Owls Event with Chloé Bakalar (AI Ethics Lead at Open AI) and Jonny Thakkar
description:
Please join Chloé Bakalar and Jonny Thakkar on Saturday April 11th, from 8pm to 11pm in SCI 199, for a Night Owls event on “AI and the Future of Humanity”.

As artificial intelligence models become more and more powerful, it is hard not to feel some degree of panic. The value of the skills we have worked so hard to acquire, the achievements we have worked so hard to accomplish, the institutions we have worked so hard to build—all that seems to be threatened with irrelevance or extinction. What will the future look like for humanity in a world dominated by AI? Or is that the wrong way of framing things? And howmuch of this is hype anyway?

To discuss these questions, along with a myriad of ethical dilemmas associated with the development of artificial intelligence, we are extraordinarily fortunate to be able to welcome Chloé Bakalar, AI Ethics Lead at Open AI. Before entering the tech world, Chloé was a political and legal theorist. She received her PhD from Penn in 2014 and completed a postdoc at Princeton before becoming an assistant professor at Temple in 2018. In 2019 she then became Chief Ethicist at Meta, where she developed and lead an applied ethics program focused on Meta’s family of AI-powered platforms, products and systems, before joining Open AI in 2025.

As a reminder, the Night Owls series consists of nocturnal philosophical discussions of questions that are central to the lives of students and faculty. The format involves an initial discussion with Jonny Thakkar, Associate Professor of Political Science, followed by an open Q&A. Cookies and coffee are provided. So far we have hosted Agnes Callard on "Is Ambition Ugly?", Krista Thomason on "Why Bad Feelings Make Life Good", Sa'ed Atshan on "Pacifism", Vincent Lloydon "What Makes a Good Protest?", Nikil Saval on "Becoming a Politician", Annie Stilz on “Can a People Own Land?”, Daniel Wodak on “What Does Democracy Actually Mean?”, Anastasia Berg on “What Are Children For?”, Musa Al-Gharbi on “What Was Wokeness?”, Peter Baumann on “Would Immortality Be Good?” and Becca Rothfeld on “Making Yourself Beautiful”. 

This event is sponsored by Philosophy, Political Science and The Point magazine.

title: Dresden Lecture
description:
Monday, April 13

4:15pm Refreshments; Lecture 4:30-5:30pm


The Gibbs Phenomenon: a 100 year adventure


When we try to approximate a function using waves—like sines and cosines—we expect that adding more and more waves will give a better and better match (much like how adding more and more polynomials can give a better fit). Surprisingly, this is not always the case! Near sharp jumps or edges, the approximation develops ripples that persist no matter how many waves we include. This puzzling behavior, now known as the Gibbs phenomenon, was first noticed in the 1800s, and was the subject of a spirited discussion in a letter to Nature in 1898.

In this talk, we will explore the history of the Gibbs phenomenon. We will try to understand why these oscillations occur and what they tell us about the limits of representing complicated signals using smooth building blocks. Along the way, we will connect this phenomenon to familiar ideas such as image compression, audio signals, and the appearance of ringing artifacts. We will then discuss how mathematicians have learned to reduce or control these oscillations. 

This talk requires no specialized background and aims to give an intuitive understanding of a phenomenon that sits at the intersection of mathematics, science, and everyday technology.

Tuesday, April 14
4:15pm Refreshments; Lecture 4:30-5:30pm

Strong stability preserving methods for time-dependent problems

When solving time-dependent problems on a computer—especially those involving sharp changes like shock waves or steep gradients—we want numerical methods that behave in a stable and reliable way. Strong stability preserving (SSP) Runge–Kutta methods are designed to do exactly this: they help ensure that important features of the solution, such as avoiding spurious oscillations, are maintained as the computation moves forward in time.

In this talk, we will introduce the basic ideas behind SSP methods and explain why they are useful in practice. We will also discuss some of the challenges in designing these methods, including limitations on how accurate or efficient they can be. Finally, we will give a broad overview of different types of SSP methods and how they are used, highlighting both what is currently possible and what questions remain open. The goal of this talk is to provide an intuitive understanding of SSP methods and help students see how modern numerical analysis tackles real computational problems.

This topic will be accessible to anyone who has taken Calculus and seen a differential equation.

title: What is the Translation Workshop (class in Fall 2026)?
description:
A meeting to learn about the Translation Workshop, scheduled to be taught by Professor Sibelan Forrester on Wednesdays from 1:15-4:00 (with a snack break) this fall. The class will combine 1) reading and discussing works on translation history from two millennia and chapters on translation theory, 2) in-class exercises including experiments with various AI interventions, and 3) workshopping translations in progress in small groups.

 

Any language with a written tradition may be used, or multiple languages if a student knows more; students should have, at a minimum, intermediate reading proficiency (or else access to a VERY patient native or proficient speaker) - about the equivalent of finishing an 004 (fourth-semester) language class at Swarthmore College. How can a professor grade translations from ANY language? - The grade will be based on the difference between the rough draft and the final draft, as well as contributions to discussion and contributions to the small group work. The outcome will be both some entertaining bilingual (multilingual) readings at the end of the fall and a portfolio of work that could then be submitted for publication.

 

Students may register for LING 070 (in which case the final portfolio must include a paper on an issue from Linguistics alongside translations that illustrate that issue), LITR 070R (final portfolio may be a paper plus related translations OR a longer set of translations), or RUSS 070 (final portfolio may involve a paper plus translations or only translations, but must be working from Russian).

 

Questions, or can't make it in person? Contact Professor Forrester at <sforres1@swarthmore.edu>.

title: Judd Kessler Lecture
description:
Department of Economics

Judd B. Kessler, Ph.D.
Howard Marks Professor
Wharton School, University of Pennsylvania 

Lucky by Design
Book Talk

Wharton economist Judd Kessler discusses his new book, “Lucky by Design: The Hidden Economics You Need to Get More of What You Want.”

Professor Kessler pulls back the curtain on the hidden markets that determine who gets what in everyday life. Unlike familiar markets that you might learn about in your economics class — where what we get depends on how much we’re willing to pay — hidden markets do not rely on prices: you can’t buy your way into a better position. Instead, what you receive hinges on the rules by which the market operates, and the choices you make in them.

What are the tricks for getting reservations at the hottest restaurants, live-event tickets, job offers, and spots in elite preschools and selective colleges? What about finding a soulmate on a dating app or receiving a life-saving organ transplant? How can policymakers design these markets better?

Professor Kessler has spent a career studying and designing these very markets. Now, he reveals insights about how they work, how to maneuver in them, and how to tip the scales in your favor.

Complimentary copies of Professor Kessler's book will be available on a first-come, first-served basis.

title: Acting Up in Hopeless Times Sager Symposium
description:
Acting Up in Hopeless Times

Sager Symposium

April 14

Kohlberg Hall, Scheuer Room

 

Schedule of Events


  • 9:55-11:10 a.m. - Panel 1:  Creating Protest/Resistance from Public Panic (Marlon Bailey, Jamal Batts, Salonee Bhanam) + moderated Q&A
  • 2:40-3:55 p.m. - Panel 2: Queer Historical Imagination in Crisis (Debra Levine, Dahlia Li, Olivia Polk) + moderated Q&A 
  • 7:00-9:30 p.m. - Activist Teach In and Q&A with ACT UP member, Debra Levine

Please RSVP for this event by 3:00 p.m. on April 7th.

Acting Up in Hopeless Times convenes an intergenerational group of public intellectuals, community historians, activists, and scholars to consider the ongoing queer legacies of the 1980’s AIDS pandemic and ACT UP activism. This symposium puts the politically-dire situation of 2026 for LGBTQ+ individuals in continuity with the political histories begun and continued in the 1980’s AIDS epidemic. By framing LGBTQ+ activism, culture, and community as the direct result of political crisis, rather than its pre-emption or remedy, this symposium aims to catalyze a renewal of 1) queer theory and historiography within scholarly and public discourses and 2) forms of queer civic and political life that have faltered since the discontinuities of knowledge transfer created by COVID-19. This event is free, accessible and open to the public.

To see full event details, please visit our website.

title: Dresden Lecture
description:
Monday, April 13

4:15pm Refreshments; Lecture 4:30-5:30pm


The Gibbs Phenomenon: a 100 year adventure


When we try to approximate a function using waves—like sines and cosines—we expect that adding more and more waves will give a better and better match (much like how adding more and more polynomials can give a better fit). Surprisingly, this is not always the case! Near sharp jumps or edges, the approximation develops ripples that persist no matter how many waves we include. This puzzling behavior, now known as the Gibbs phenomenon, was first noticed in the 1800s, and was the subject of a spirited discussion in a letter to Nature in 1898.

In this talk, we will explore the history of the Gibbs phenomenon. We will try to understand why these oscillations occur and what they tell us about the limits of representing complicated signals using smooth building blocks. Along the way, we will connect this phenomenon to familiar ideas such as image compression, audio signals, and the appearance of ringing artifacts. We will then discuss how mathematicians have learned to reduce or control these oscillations. 

This talk requires no specialized background and aims to give an intuitive understanding of a phenomenon that sits at the intersection of mathematics, science, and everyday technology.

Tuesday, April 14
4:15pm Refreshments; Lecture 4:30-5:30pm

Strong stability preserving methods for time-dependent problems

When solving time-dependent problems on a computer—especially those involving sharp changes like shock waves or steep gradients—we want numerical methods that behave in a stable and reliable way. Strong stability preserving (SSP) Runge–Kutta methods are designed to do exactly this: they help ensure that important features of the solution, such as avoiding spurious oscillations, are maintained as the computation moves forward in time.

In this talk, we will introduce the basic ideas behind SSP methods and explain why they are useful in practice. We will also discuss some of the challenges in designing these methods, including limitations on how accurate or efficient they can be. Finally, we will give a broad overview of different types of SSP methods and how they are used, highlighting both what is currently possible and what questions remain open. The goal of this talk is to provide an intuitive understanding of SSP methods and help students see how modern numerical analysis tackles real computational problems.

This topic will be accessible to anyone who has taken Calculus and seen a differential equation.

Please join us this academic year for the Lunch and Learn Series 2025-2026. Focusing on Healing and Re-building Community, we have invited Creative Praxis to Swarthmore to guide us through this year's series. 

 

Creative Praxis is an arts-based, healing-centered, and joy-filled training organization. Their programs are designed to inspire and connect people, fostering personal and community transformation. With over 60 years of combined experience, their facilitators are experts in interactive, transformational facilitation. The founder, Nia Eubanks-Dixon, is certified in restorative practices, trauma healing, and de-escalation techniques.

 

September 11th - Moving Through Conflict with Clarity and Care  - An interactive session that challenges the fear of conflict for growth and connection. Learn culturally relevant strategies to navigate conflict with empathy, compassion, and clarity. When conflict is approached with intention, honesty, and care, we strengthen relationships and build healthier communities.

title: Fetter Chamber Music Concert
description:
The Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program and the Department of Music present student musicians from the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Program in a series of chamber music concerts. 

The Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program has been an integral part of the Swarthmore College performance program for four decades. It began in 1975 as the ""Pollard Scholarship Funds,"" with an initial contribution from Elizabeth Pollard Fetter '25 in memory of her mother, Emilie Garrett Pollard '93. With the passing of Elizabeth Pollard Fetter in 1977, husband Frank Whitson Fetter '20 stepped forward to endow the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter String Quartet Scholarships at Swarthmore College. Dozens of student musicians were subsequently supported in their desire to explore the string quartet repertoire.

In 2001, the program was renamed the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program and was expanded to support the coaching of multiple chamber music groups. Funding continues to be enhanced by successive generations of the Fetter family, including Bob Fetter ’53, Tom Fetter ’56, Ellen Fetter, and Annie Fetter ’88. Swarthmore College, students and faculty past and present, and the community at large, has been enriched by the musical communication made possible through the generosity of the Fetter family.

This performance is free and open to the public.  Seating is first come first served. This performance will be streamed live.

title: Environmental Studies Program Colloquium Series: Kyle Whyte
description:
Environmental Studies Program 2026 Colloquium Series

Talk by: Kyle Whyte     

Date:  Monday, April 20th, 2026
 
Time:  12:00 pm - 1:00 pm  (Bring your lunch)

 
Location:  SCI Cunniff (SC 199)

Title: "Why Does Anything Need to be Called Wild?" **



The impacts of climate change and biodiversity loss are creating extreme unease about the future of natural places. Moreover, groups such as Indigenous peoples are facing some of the most acute harms. Yet, is wilderness the best term to call these places? This presentation will discuss why Indigenous peoples have alternative ways to conceptualize climate mitigation and biodiversity conservation that need not lean on the use of the term "wilderness." 
 

** Kyle Whyte, "Why Does Anything Need to be Called Wild?", In, The Heart of the Wild: Essays on Nature, Conservation, and the Human Future, edited by Ben A. Minteer and Jonathan B. Loso (Princeton University Press, 2024).

 


 

 


Kyle Whyte is the George Willis Pack Professor in the School for Environment and Sustainability and University Diversity and a Social Transformation Professor. He teaches in and coordinates the School’s environmental justice graduate specialization. He is founding Faculty Director of the Tishman Center for Social Justice and the Environment, Principal Investigator of the Environmental Justice + Humanities Hub, co-Principal Investigator of the Global Center for Climate Change and Transboundary Waters, Faculty Associate of Native American Studies, Principal Investigator of the Secretariat for the Pathways Alliance for Change and Transformation, STRIDE Committee member, affiliate Professor of Philosophy, and Senior Fellow in the Michigan Society of Fellows. Whyte’s research on environmental justice centers on moral and political issues that Indigenous peoples are addressing in the areas of climate change, conservation, and cooperative relationships with science institutions. He is an enrolled member of the Citizen Potawatomi Nation. His publications appear in journals including Climatic Change, Weather, Climate & Society, Science, Daedalus, WIREs Climate Change, Environment & Planning E, and Sustainability Science.


 


Co-sponsored by the Environmental Justice & Community Resilience Program at the Lang Center, Environmental Studies Program, Office of Sustainability and the Mellon Foundation.

title: Dr. Kyle Whyte Lecture/ “How Does Indigenous Peoples' Climate Action Scale Up?”
description: Indigenous peoples’ actions to address climate change continue to achieve meaningful effects, impacting the United Nations, multinational industries, regional governance, and national laws, policies, and investments. Yet many people assume that only multilateral institutions and nations can move the needle at global and regional scales. Indigenous peoples are often labeled as actors who can make change slowly and at local scales. The assumption that Indigenous actions cannot achieve results 'at scale' is wrong. Indigenous movements show how responsible relationships, kinship bonds, care for land, and self-determination are effective elements of methods for scaling up. Compared to dominant methods of scaling up, which are replete with tradeoffs that trend toward worsening climate risk, carbon footprints, and human suffering, Indigenous methods are ethical, just, and can be organized to achieve speed.

title: The Harvard School of Meaning- Centered Medical Anthropology: Reflections After 50 Years
description:
The Harvard School of Meaning-Centered Medical Anthropology: Reflections After 50 Years


 

WHEN: Tuesday, April 21, 2026, 4:30 pm

WHERE: Science Center 101

 

Why should meaning-centered Medical Anthropology matter for undergraduates, social scientists, and educated people globally? This talk uses the story of Harvard's five decades of medical anthropological teaching, research and practice to illustrate why a meaning-centered approach to experience is so vital for our times. That approach creates a framework for the practical application of wisdom derived from the human sciences and puts it to work to reimagine our social condition and develop more availing approaches and practices. This talk is dedicated to a former student and leader of the Harvard program: Paul Farmer, whose brilliance and moral passion informed our emphasis on accompaniment, human rights, and integration of the moral, the emotional and the practical.

Description:

This is the third in a series of events on the renaming of Old Science Hall. 

Lisa Alvarez-Cohen, an environmental engineer whose research focuses on the application of microbiological processes for the elimination of environmental contaminants, was named Berkeley’s vice provost for academic planning in 2018. Last year, Berkeley chose an Indigenous name for a campus building and consulted with Indigenous people in the process.

 

Kimberly Wright Cassidy ’85, H’25, joined Bryn Mawr’s psychology faculty in 1993 and served as its ninth president from 2013-2024. During her tenure as president, she oversaw the renaming of Bryn Mawr’s library.  

 

Cat Norris is Associate Dean of the Faculty for Academic Programs and Research and an associate professor of psychology. She joined Swarthmore’s faculty in 2013.

title: Narrating the Goddess: Professor Jessica Birkenholtz's Translation on Swasthani Vrata Kartha
description: The Swasthani Vrata Katha is Nepal’s most widely read and recited Hindu devotional text. Dating to the late 16th century, many Nepali Hindus annually perform a month-long recitation of it still today. It is a collection of widely circulating Hindu myths about gods, demons, and divine dalliances that forefront the great god Shiva and the goddesses Sati and Parvati. It is also a local folk story about women’s real, everyday hardships and the rewards of attending to one’s dharma and devotion to the goddess Swasthani. In this talk, Professor Birkenholtz draws on her decades-long study of the Swasthani to reflect, first, on how the Swasthani both prescribes idealized womanhood for Nepali women and illustrates many lived realities. Second, she will consider how the portrayal and treatment of women is variously experienced as misogynistic or empowering among Nepali women. Finally, Professor Birkenholtz will share her experience as a feminist scholar of religion of translating the Swasthani into English with a female Nepali scholar and collaborator.

Swarthmore Night at Lincoln Center Theater: Ragtime

Lincoln Center Theater

This event has reached capacity. To be added to a waitlist, please contact alumni@swarthmore.edu.

We’re coming together to experience Ragtime, a breathtaking musical adaptation of E.L. Doctorow’s celebrated novel. title: Senior Lang Scholar Presentations
description:
Launched in the early 80s at Swarthmore College by Eugene M. Lang ’38, the Lang Opportunity Scholarship Program selects up to six members of the sophomore class each year and provides them with an array of undergraduate and graduate benefits – including a $12k project grant.

To date, LOS has cultivated 100s of Lang Scholars who have partnered with 100s more communities on projects all over the world. Join the LOS Class of 2026 to hear about their inspiring projects! They are:


  • Mya Chuluundorj's project, “Story-Capturers: The Voices of the Land,” is a digital archive preserving the cultural heritage of Mongolian rural communities, engaging students against climate change and urbanization.  
  • Lena Habtu created a documentary with Human Rights First Ethiopia highlighting Tigrayans’ experiences during the 2020-2022 conflict.
  • Prerna Karmacharya’s project seeks to decrease the underrepresentation of blind and visually impaired individuals in STEM fields like robotics in partnership with Perkins School for the Blind. 
  • Steven Mukum’s project, Rising Above Water, addresses internal displacement in Dangbo, Benin, caused by climate-related flooding through training in sustainable agriculture and providing flood-resistant infrastructure in partnership with OJECC Bénin.
  • Billy Wu’s project, Acquired Tomorrows, reduces stigma and increases access to mental health with project partner, Home for the Disabled Kunshan and ShangXiang Counseling.

title: SSQL Workshop: Principles of Reproducible Research
description: This workshop will focus on practical methods for making your research transparent, reliable, and reproducible. You will learn key techniques for organizing data and analysis, documenting your workflow, and using tools like R Markdown and version control to ensure that your results can be easily verified and updated. We will cover best practices for sharing data and code, strategies for collaborative research, and common pitfalls to avoid. By the end of the session, you’ll have the skills and resources to make your research more open and trustworthy from start to finish.

title: Senior Case 2026
description:
The Senior Showcase provides a space where the Swarthmore community comes together to learn from senior projects across all three divisions. Participating students each have three minutes and one slide to present their work. Why only three minutes when our students could spend hours sharing the details of their projects? Most people will not take more than three minutes to listen and understand a student's work; therefore, students need to learn both how to distill their work into a three-minute sound bite and how to explain their work to a more general audience. Three minutes also allows for more participation from interested seniors.

title: Fetter Chamber Music Concert
description:
The Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program and the Department of Music present student musicians from the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Program in a series of chamber music concerts. 

The Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program has been an integral part of the Swarthmore College performance program for four decades. It began in 1975 as the ""Pollard Scholarship Funds,"" with an initial contribution from Elizabeth Pollard Fetter '25 in memory of her mother, Emilie Garrett Pollard '93. With the passing of Elizabeth Pollard Fetter in 1977, husband Frank Whitson Fetter '20 stepped forward to endow the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter String Quartet Scholarships at Swarthmore College. Dozens of student musicians were subsequently supported in their desire to explore the string quartet repertoire.

In 2001, the program was renamed the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program and was expanded to support the coaching of multiple chamber music groups. Funding continues to be enhanced by successive generations of the Fetter family, including Bob Fetter ’53, Tom Fetter ’56, Ellen Fetter, and Annie Fetter ’88. Swarthmore College, students and faculty past and present, and the community at large, has been enriched by the musical communication made possible through the generosity of the Fetter family.

This performance is free and open to the public.  Seating is first come first served. This performance will be streamed live.

title: Arabic Language Table
description: The Arabic Language Table provides a relaxed, supportive space for students to practice conversational Arabic, build confidence, and engage with the language beyond the classroom. It fosters spontaneous speech, reinforces vocabulary in context, and encourages cultural exploration through discussions on music, films, and current events. By creating a sense of community, it connects students across proficiency levels, motivating them to participate and apply their skills in real-world interactions. This informal setting helps bridge classroom learning with practical use, preparing students for study abroad, internships, and conversations with native speakers. Ultimately, it enhances fluency, cultural awareness, and a deeper appreciation for the Arabic language.

Description:

This is the fourth and final event in a series on the renaming of Old Science Hall.

 

Jeremy Johnson is the cultural education director of the Delaware Tribe of Indians, based in Bartlesville, Okla. He is Lenape, Absentee Shawnee, and Peoria. Prior to his current role, he served as assistant chief of the Delaware Tribe. A lifelong educator, he is committed to preserving and revitalizing Lenape culture and language for the future generations of his tribe.

title: Fetter Chamber Music Concert
description:
The Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program and the Department of Music present student musicians from the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Program in a series of chamber music concerts. 

The Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program has been an integral part of the Swarthmore College performance program for four decades. It began in 1975 as the ""Pollard Scholarship Funds,"" with an initial contribution from Elizabeth Pollard Fetter '25 in memory of her mother, Emilie Garrett Pollard '93. With the passing of Elizabeth Pollard Fetter in 1977, husband Frank Whitson Fetter '20 stepped forward to endow the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter String Quartet Scholarships at Swarthmore College. Dozens of student musicians were subsequently supported in their desire to explore the string quartet repertoire.

In 2001, the program was renamed the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program and was expanded to support the coaching of multiple chamber music groups. Funding continues to be enhanced by successive generations of the Fetter family, including Bob Fetter ’53, Tom Fetter ’56, Ellen Fetter, and Annie Fetter ’88. Swarthmore College, students and faculty past and present, and the community at large, has been enriched by the musical communication made possible through the generosity of the Fetter family.

This performance is free and open to the public.  Seating is first come first served. This performance will be streamed live.

description:
Step outside and connect with your local community through SwatWalks, our new alumni-led series of outdoor social strolls! We are kicking off our very first outing in the Bay Area with a walk around Oakland's Lake Merritt.

Join host Margaret Huang '87 and fellow alumni for our inaugural SwatWalks: Oakland — a casual, family-friendly walk around scenic Lake Merritt, the nation's first wildlife refuge.

title: Pilates with Cathy Raymond
description:
Pilates is a progressive system of exercises designed to stretch, strengthen, and balance both mind and body. Benefits include increased core strength, better posture and balance skills, more physical and mental energy, and improved sports performance.

A recording of each class is available on YouTube.

title: Garnet Day
description:
Welcome the long-awaited springtime with music and dance! 

This Garnet Day, stop by the beautiful new Arts Plaza behind Martin Hall between 10:30 a.m. and 12:30 p.m. to enjoy musical and dance performances from our talented student artists. There will also be treats from The Chilly Banana food truck (vegan options available) and the chance to talk to folks from Get Out the Vote, the Office of Sustainability, the Swarthmore Coalition on Well-Being, Student Disability Services, and the Inclusive Excellence Fellows Initiatives.

Garnet Day Springtime Music and Dance Celebration

Arts Plaza, Behind Martin Hall

Thursday, April 2

10:30 a.m. – 12:30 p.m.

Rain location Lang Concert Hall and Lobby

We hope you can join us in celebrating the warmer weather and our talented community members. As always, remember to wear your Swarthmore gear to show your spirit!

Congratulations to last month's Garnet Day Guessing Game winners: Charlotte Dustin ‘29 (first place), Kat Matekovic (second place), and Amanda Whitbred, Communications (third place)! There were 1745 pieces of candy in the jar.
location: Parrish East Parlors - Piano, Parrish Lawn - Beach, Parrish West Parlors, Front Lawn of Pearson, Old Science Hall Lawn, Foulk Walk, Singer First Floor Commons, Singer Terrace
